1→# Stack Research
     2→
     3→**Domain:** LoRA episodic memory system for local coding agents
     4→**Researched:** 2026-03-02
     5→**Confidence:** MEDIUM — most findings verified against official sources; Blackwell compatibility is the primary uncertainty zone
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Critical Context: RTX 5090 / Blackwell (sm_120) Support Status
    10→
    11→The RTX 5090 uses NVIDIA Blackwell architecture at compute capability sm_120. As of March 2026, **sm_120 support is actively being resolved but not fully stable across all libraries.** This is the dominant engineering constraint for the entire stack. The working path requires:
    12→
    13→- CUDA 12.8 (cu128) — official NVIDIA toolkit, fully sm_120-compatible
    14→- PyTorch nightly or 2.9.0.dev+cu128 — **stable PyTorch 2.7/2.8 has no cu128 wheels, creating a dependency deadlock**
    15→- Triton >= 3.3.1 — the first Triton version with official sm_120 support
    16→- Source-built vLLM — PyPI wheels lack sm_120 support as of this writing
    17→- Unsloth's Docker image — the cleanest path to a working Blackwell fine-tuning stack
    18→
    19→This constraint propagates to every library below. Where a library lacks confirmed sm_120 wheels, note the workaround.
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→## Recommended Stack
    24→
    25→### Core Technologies
    26→
    27→| Technology | Version | Purpose | Why Recommended |
    28→|------------|---------|---------|-----------------|
    29→| PyTorch | 2.9.0.dev+cu128 (nightly) | Tensor ops, autograd, model backbone | Only PyTorch build with CUDA 12.8 wheels that expose sm_120; stable 2.7/2.8 lack cu128 wheels entirely |
    30→| CUDA Toolkit | 12.8 | GPU compilation target | First toolkit to fully expose sm_120 (Blackwell) compute primitives; CUDA 12.9 may be needed for CUTLASS 3.9 (required for FP8 on sm_120) |
    31→| Triton | >= 3.3.1 | GPU kernel compilation (used by PyTorch/vLLM) | First release with official sm_120 support; required by Unsloth on Blackwell |
    32→| vLLM | 0.16.x (source build) | Multi-LoRA inference server | Most mature production LoRA serving engine as of Feb 2026; v0.15+ has 454% OTPS improvement for multi-LoRA; PyPI wheels do not include sm_120 — must build from source with cu128 |
    33→| PEFT | 0.18.1 | LoRA adapter definition, training, merging | HuggingFace standard for LoRA/QLoRA; compatible with Transformers v5; actively maintained |
    34→| bitsandbytes | 0.49.2 | 4-bit quantization (QLoRA) | Ships cu128 wheels natively (confirmed by Unsloth docs); enables QLoRA on Blackwell without source build |
    35→| Transformers (HuggingFace) | 5.x (5.2+ recommended) | Model loading, tokenization, base architecture | v5 released Jan 2026; PyTorch-first, quantization as first-class feature; weekly minor releases |
    36→| Unsloth | latest | LoRA/QLoRA training on Blackwell | The only battle-tested fine-tuning stack with documented RTX 5090 support; wraps PEFT/bitsandbytes/triton; Docker image is the cleanest entry point |
    37→
    38→### LoRA Serving Layer
    39→
    40→| Technology | Version | Purpose | Why Recommended |
    41→|------------|---------|---------|-----------------|
    42→| vLLM multi-LoRA | 0.15.0+ | Multi-adapter inference serving | Absorbed S-LoRA techniques (Punica kernels); serves concurrent adapters; 0.16.x is current stable; use over standalone S-LoRA |
    43→| SGLang | latest (0.4.x) | Alternative inference engine for agent workloads | 29% faster than vLLM in 2026 benchmarks; better for structured/agent workloads via RadixAttention; multi-LoRA batching built in via S-LoRA/Punica integration |
    44→| LoRAX (Predibase) | latest (GitHub main) | Alternative: heterogeneous multi-adapter serving | Open source (Apache 2.0); adapter exchange scheduling + CPU offload; last commit May 2025; useful if vLLM/SGLang multi-LoRA has gaps |
    45→
    46→### Hypernetwork (Doc-to-LoRA)
    47→
    48→| Technology | Version | Purpose | Why Recommended |
    49→|------------|---------|---------|-----------------|
    50→| SakanaAI/doc-to-lora | main (MIT license) | Reference implementation of Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork | Official Sakana AI implementation; open source MIT; uv-managed; targets Gemma models but architecture is HuggingFace-generic; published Feb 2026 |
    51→| SHINE (Yewei-Liu/SHINE) | main | Alternative: transformer-based in-context hypernetwork | Concurrent Feb 2026 work; uses frozen LLM's own representations as hypernetwork; single forward pass; code available |
    52→| HuggingFace Accelerate | latest | Multi-GPU training distribution | Required by doc-to-lora for multi-GPU; standard for distributed training across dual 5090s |
    53→
    54→### KV Cache and Memory
    55→
    56→| Technology | Version | Purpose | Why Recommended |
    57→|------------|---------|---------|-----------------|
    58→| LMCache | latest (0.x) | Cross-request KV cache reuse | 3-10x latency reduction on multi-turn workloads (agent loops); vLLM + SGLang integration; 15x throughput improvement on RAG-style tasks; Q1 2026 roadmap targets stability |
    59→| vLLM prefix caching | built-in | In-engine KV prefix reuse | Free with vLLM; covers the simple case (repeated system prompts, shared prefixes); use before adding LMCache overhead |
    60→
    61→### Supporting Libraries
    62→
    63→| Library | Version | Purpose | When to Use |
    64→|---------|---------|---------|-------------|
    65→| Flash Attention | >= 2.x (custom wheels for sm_120) | Efficient attention kernels | Official FA wheels do NOT support sm_120; use community-compiled wheels or SageAttention; FA4 targets sm_100 (H100) only |
    66→| SageAttention | latest | Blackwell-compatible attention alternative | THU-ML project; sm_120 support being added; use as FA fallback on 5090s |
    67→| safetensors | latest | Fast, safe model weight serialization | Required for adapter storage; standard in HuggingFace ecosystem |
    68→| datasets (HuggingFace) | latest | Training data pipeline | Standard for loading/streaming code corpora |
    69→| wandb or mlflow | latest | Experiment tracking for hypernetwork training | Track hypernetwork training runs; mlflow ships with the monorepo template |
    70→| FastAPI | latest | API layer for Rune agent | Already in monorepo template; serves agent orchestration endpoints |
    71→| LangGraph | latest | Agent orchestration / System-2 loop | Already in monorepo template; handles the reasoning loop state machine |
    72→
    73→### Development Tools
    74→
    75→| Tool | Purpose | Notes |
    76→|------|---------|-------|
    77→| uv | Python package/project management | Already mandated in project; doc-to-lora also uses uv; use `uv run` for all commands |
    78→| Docker + NVIDIA Container Toolkit | Blackwell-compatible runtime | Unsloth's Docker image is the recommended base for sm_120 training; CUDA 12.8 base image |
    79→| nvidia-smi / nvitop | GPU monitoring | Verify sm_120 detection and VRAM allocation across dual 5090s |
    80→| pytest | Testing | Standard; already in monorepo template |
    81→
    82→---
    83→
    84→## Installation
    85→
    86→```bash
    87→# 1. Use CUDA 12.8 base (Docker recommended for Blackwell)
    88→# Base image: nvidia/cuda:12.8.1-cudnn-devel-ubuntu22.04
    89→
    90→# 2. Install PyTorch nightly with cu128 (REQUIRED for sm_120)
    91→uv pip install --pre torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/nightly/cu128
    92→
    93→# 3. Install Triton 3.3.1+ (Blackwell sm_120 support)
    94→uv pip install triton>=3.3.1
    95→
    96→# 4. Install bitsandbytes (has cu128 wheels natively)
    97→uv pip install bitsandbytes>=0.49.2
    98→
    99→# 5. Install PEFT and Transformers v5
   100→uv pip install peft>=0.18.1 transformers>=5.0.0
   101→
   102→# 6. Install HuggingFace Accelerate for multi-GPU
   103→uv pip install accelerate
   104→
   105→# 7. Clone and build vLLM from source (PyPI wheel lacks sm_120)
   106→git clone https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm
   107→cd vllm && python use_existing_torch.py
   108→TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST="12.0" VLLM_FLASH_ATTN_VERSION=2 MAX_JOBS=6 \
   109→  uv pip install --no-build-isolation -e .
   110→
   111→# 8. Install Unsloth (for training pipeline)
   112→# Use Unsloth's Docker image or their documented Blackwell install path
   113→
   114→# 9. Clone Doc-to-LoRA reference implementation
   115→git clone https://github.com/SakanaAI/doc-to-lora
   116→cd doc-to-lora && ./install.sh  # uses uv internally
   117→
   118→# 10. Install LMCache
   119→uv pip install lmcache
   120→
   121→# 11. Supporting libraries
   122→uv pip install safetensors datasets wandb
   123→```
   124→
   125→---
   126→
   127→## Alternatives Considered
   128→
   129→| Recommended | Alternative | Why Not |
   130→|-------------|-------------|---------|
   131→| vLLM (source build, 0.16.x) | S-LoRA standalone | S-LoRA (github.com/S-LoRA/S-LoRA) built on LightLLM, not updated since 2024; vLLM absorbed equivalent Punica kernels; use vLLM instead |
   132→| vLLM | LoRAX (Predibase) | LoRAX is viable fallback (Apache 2.0, active as of May 2025) but vLLM has larger ecosystem and more recent optimization; use LoRAX if heterogeneous adapter exchange scheduling is critical |
   133→| SGLang | vLLM (for agent workloads) | SGLang is 29% faster in 2026 benchmarks and better for structured agent outputs; serious alternative if agent loop performance is the bottleneck |
   134→| Unsloth | Raw PEFT + bitsandbytes | Unsloth is the only stack with documented RTX 5090 Blackwell support and working Docker images; raw PEFT + bitsandbytes requires manual sm_120 path resolution |
   135→| Doc-to-LoRA (SakanaAI) | SHINE hypernetwork | SHINE (arXiv:2602.06358) is concurrent Feb 2026 work with a different architecture (uses frozen LLM's own representations); both are viable; Doc-to-LoRA has cleaner reference implementation and MIT license |
   136→| bitsandbytes QLoRA | GPTQ quantization | GPTQ doesn't have confirmed sm_120 wheels; bitsandbytes is confirmed working on Blackwell via Unsloth |
   137→| LMCache | vLLM prefix caching only | vLLM's built-in prefix cache handles simple cases; LMCache adds cross-request and cross-session reuse which matters for a long-running coding agent |
   138→| PyTorch nightly (cu128) | TensorFlow / JAX | TF is dead for this use case; JAX/XLA has even less Blackwell support than PyTorch |
   139→
   140→---
   141→
   142→## What NOT to Use
   143→
   144→| Avoid | Why | Use Instead |
   145→|-------|-----|-------------|
   146→| Stable PyTorch 2.7.x or 2.8.x | No cu128 wheels exist; creates a dependency deadlock for sm_120 support | PyTorch 2.9.0.dev nightly with cu128 |
   147→| vLLM from PyPI | Binary wheels do not include sm_120 CUTLASS kernels; will fail on FP8/quantized ops on 5090 | Build vLLM from source with `TORCH_CUDA_ARCH_LIST="12.0"` |
   148→| Flash Attention official wheels | FA2 and FA4 do not support sm_120; FA4 targets sm_100 (Hopper/H100) only; raises RuntimeError on 5090 | Community-compiled FA wheels or SageAttention |
   149→| S-LoRA standalone (github.com/S-LoRA/S-LoRA) | Unmaintained; built on LightLLM (not vLLM); the multi-LoRA techniques have been absorbed into vLLM | vLLM 0.15.0+ multi-LoRA or SGLang |
   150→| PEFT 0.17.x or older | Pre-dates Transformers v5 compatibility fix in 0.18.1; use the patch release | peft>=0.18.1 |
   151→| GGUF/llama.cpp for serving | llama.cpp does not support dynamic LoRA swapping needed for episodic memory | vLLM or SGLang with LoRA serving |
   152→| Ollama | No programmatic multi-LoRA API; not suitable for the adapter lifecycle management Rune requires | vLLM with OpenAI-compatible API |
   153→| Windows host | sm_120 + CUDA 12.8 + PyTorch nightly support is Linux-only in early 2026; WSL2 has additional CUTLASS issues | Native Linux or Docker on Linux |
   154→
   155→---
   156→
   157→## Stack Patterns by Variant
   158→
   159→**For training the hypernetwork (Doc-to-LoRA)**:
   160→- Use Unsloth's Docker image as base
   161→- PyTorch nightly (cu128) + Triton 3.3.1+ + bitsandbytes 0.49.2
   162→- Accelerate for multi-GPU distribution across both 5090s
   163→- PEFT 0.18.1 for adapter management during training
   164→- SakanaAI/doc-to-lora reference code as the starting point
   165→- Target Qwen2.5-Coder-7B or DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B as base model (strong coding benchmarks, <10B, fits in QLoRA on single 5090)
   166→
   167→**For inference/serving (LoRA swap at agent runtime)**:
   168→- vLLM (source build, 0.16.x) with multi-LoRA enabled, OR SGLang 0.4.x
   169→- LMCache layered on top for KV cache reuse across agent loop turns
   170→- One 5090 for base model + adapter serving; second 5090 for hypernetwork or tensor parallel
   171→- `enable-lora`, `max-loras`, `max-cpu-loras` flags to control adapter pool
   172→
   173→**For base model selection (<10B, dual RTX 5090 target)**:
   174→- Primary: Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct (61.6% HumanEval; HuggingFace-native; strong LoRA ecosystem)
   175→- Alternative: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B (better math/reasoning at 7B; same Qwen backbone, same fine-tuning path)
   176→- Both fit comfortably in QLoRA on a single 5090 (32GB VRAM) with ~16-20GB footprint
   177→
   178→---
   179→
   180→## Version Compatibility
   181→
   182→| Package | Compatible With | Notes |
   183→|---------|-----------------|-------|
   184→| peft==0.18.1 | transformers>=5.0.0 | 0.18.1 was specifically released for v5 compatibility |
   185→| bitsandbytes==0.49.2 | CUDA 12.8, sm_120 | Ships cu128 wheels; confirmed by Unsloth Blackwell docs |
   186→| triton>=3.3.1 | sm_120 (RTX 5090) | First version with official Blackwell support; required by Unsloth |
   187→| torch nightly (cu128) | CUDA 12.8 only | No stable wheel with cu128 exists as of March 2026; nightly is the only option |
   188→| vLLM (source, main) | torch nightly (cu128) | Must be source-built against the same cu128 PyTorch; set `VLLM_FLASH_ATTN_VERSION=2` |
   189→| lmcache | vLLM or SGLang | Integrates as middleware; test with vLLM 0.15+ specifically |
   190→| SakanaAI/doc-to-lora | Gemma or generic HuggingFace | MIT license; uv-managed; designed for Transformers-compatible models |
   191→| flash-attention (official) | sm_80, sm_86, sm_89, sm_90 ONLY | Does NOT support sm_120; use community wheels or SageAttention |
   192→
   193→---
   194→
   195→## Base Model Recommendation
   196→
   197→**Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct** is the recommended base for Rune's first milestone because:
   198→- 7B fits in QLoRA (4-bit) with ~12-14GB VRAM, leaving headroom on a 32GB 5090 for the adapter and KV cache
   199→- Specifically tuned for code tasks (61.6% HumanEval, competitive across multiple coding benchmarks)
   200→- Native HuggingFace support: PEFT/vLLM/SGLang all work out of the box
   201→- Apache 2.0 license — no commercial restrictions
   202→- The Qwen architecture is the same backbone used by DeepSeek-R1 distills, so the LoRA fine-tuning recipes transfer
   203→
   204→If stronger reasoning on code is needed, **DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B** is the drop-in alternative (same VRAM profile, same fine-tuning path, better on reasoning benchmarks like AIME/MATH-500).
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## Sources

   210→- [arXiv:2602.15902 — Doc-to-LoRA paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.15902) — MEDIUM confidence (very recent, Feb 2026)
   211→- [github.com/SakanaAI/doc-to-lora](https://github.com/SakanaAI/doc-to-lora) — HIGH confidence (official implementation, MIT license)

   215→- [docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/features/lora/](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/features/lora/) — HIGH confidence (official docs)

   220→- [developer.nvidia.com — Unsloth + Blackwell guide](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/train-an-llm-on-an-nvidia-blackwell-desktop-with-unsloth-and-scale-it/) — HIGH confidence (NVIDIA official blog)

# Phase 2: Implementation Plan - Context

**Gathered:** 2026-03-02
**Status:** Ready for planning

<domain>
## Phase Boundary

Write a detailed, phased implementation plan that transforms the Rune architecture specification into actionable phases with pass/fail criteria, risk gates, and a concrete build order grounded in hardware reality. Output is documentation (Markdown files served via MkDocs), not code. The plan covers the full journey from hardware validation through hypernetwork training.

</domain>

<decisions>
## Implementation Decisions

### Plan granularity
- 5-6 coarse implementation phases (not 8-10 fine-grained ones)
- Each phase gets detailed treatment: goal paragraph, rationale for ordering, deliverables, dependencies, risk callouts, and success criteria (~30-40 lines per phase)
- No time estimates — ordering and dependencies only. Timelines are speculative at research stage.
- Single kill-switch gate at Phase 1 (hypothesis validation). Later phases have success criteria but are not hard stops.

### Document structure
- Hybrid tone: engineering spec for phase structure (deliverables, criteria, dependencies) with paper-adjacent framing for context and rationale sections
- Main document + appendices approach: paper-adjacent main plan document plus separate engineering spec appendices (risk matrix, build order details)
- Use MkDocs (already in the repo) to serve all documentation
- Remove existing template/placeholder docs from MkDocs — leave a clean build with only real Rune documentation
- Executive summary first: 1-page overview (goal, phase count, key risks, build order), then detailed phase-by-phase breakdown
- Visual aids: Mermaid dependency graph showing phase ordering, plus tables for build order and risk matrix

### Success criteria style
- Mix of metrics and checklists: specific numbers where measurable (e.g., 5% Pass@1), binary checklists where not (e.g., "adapter registry stores and retrieves")
- Kill-switch gate: primary metric is Pass@1 improvement on HumanEval, with secondary diagnostic signals (adapter convergence, training loss) tracked via MLflow
- Use MLflow for experiment tracking throughout
- Include experiment sketches per phase: dataset, baseline, eval method, expected range — enough to start, not full protocols
- Failed criteria = stop and reassess. No predefined fallback strategies. Document what was learned, revisit approach.

### Claude's Discretion
- Exact phase boundaries and what goes in each phase (within the 5-6 phase constraint)
- Mermaid diagram layout and detail level
- How to structure the MkDocs nav for the new documentation
- Balance of detail between main document and appendices
- Specific experiment sketch parameters (dataset sizes, baseline choices)

</decisions>

<specifics>
## Specific Ideas

- MkDocs is already configured in the repo — use it natively, don't add a separate docs framework
- Template/placeholder docs from the monorepo scaffold should be removed so the docs site only contains real Rune documentation
- MLflow for experiment tracking is a firm choice — reference it in the kill-switch phase and any phase with measurable criteria
- The README (Phase 1 output) already establishes the paper-adjacent voice and Mermaid diagram conventions — the implementation plan should feel like a natural continuation

</specifics>

<deferred>
## Deferred Ideas

None — discussion stayed within phase scope

</deferred>

</phase_context>

<additional_context>
**Phase description:** ### Phase 2: Implementation Plan
**Goal**: A detailed, phased implementation plan document exists that transforms the Rune architecture specification into actionable phases with pass/fail criteria, risk gates, and a concrete build order grounded in hardware reality
**Depends on**: Phase 1
**Requirements**: PLAN-01, PLAN-02, PLAN-03, PLAN-04, PLAN-05, PLAN-06
**Success Criteria** (what must be TRUE):
  1. Phase 0 of the implementation plan defines hardware and environment validation with specific pass/fail criteria (both GPUs recognized, PyTorch nightly cu128 forward+backward pass without segfault, vLLM serving Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via pipeline parallelism without TP+LoRA corruption)
  2. Phase 1 of the implementation plan is structured as a kill-switch gate: it defines a measurable success threshold (at minimum, 5% Pass@1 improvement on held-out HumanEval tasks) and states explicitly that no infrastructure is built if this gate fails
  3. Every subsequent phase in the plan states its deliverables, which previous phase it depends on, and the success criteria that mark it complete
  4. The plan explicitly addresses the three primary research risks: adapter composition interference (heterogeneous subspaces), hypernetwork mode collapse (mean-adapter collapse), and catastrophic forgetting (non-immutable adapter writes) — each with a mitigation strategy
  5. The plan reflects the hardware reality: dual RTX 4090 with CXL, pipeline parallelism (PP=2) not tensor parallelism, QLoRA as required for 24GB per-GPU constraint, with QLoRA introduced only after Phase 1 bfloat16 baseline passes
  6. The plan includes the recommended build order: adapter-registry → lora-server → model-training extensions → api-service extensions → rune-agent → evolution-svc → training-svc → hypernetwork

**Phase requirement IDs (MUST address):** PLAN-01, PLAN-02, PLAN-03, PLAN-04, PLAN-05, PLAN-06

**Requirements:**
- PLAN-01: Phase 0 defines hardware/environment validation with specific pass/fail criteria
- PLAN-02: Phase 1 defines core hypothesis validation as kill-switch gate (Doc-to-LoRA on coding tasks)
- PLAN-03: Each subsequent phase has clear deliverables, dependencies, and success criteria
- PLAN-04: Plan addresses key research risks: adapter composition interference, hypernetwork mode collapse, catastrophic forgetting
- PLAN-05: Plan reflects hardware reality: dual RTX 4090 + CXL, pipeline parallelism (not tensor), QLoRA required
- PLAN-06: Plan includes suggested build order from architecture research (adapter-registry → lora-server → api-extensions → rune-agent → evolution → training)

**Prior decisions:**
- [Roadmap]: Documentation-first milestone — 3 phases (README, Implementation Plan, Architecture Docs)
- [Roadmap]: Phase 2 kill-switch gate: 5% Pass@1 improvement on HumanEval required before infrastructure build
- [Roadmap]: Pipeline parallelism (PP=2, TP=1) mandated — TP+LoRA confirmed bug on consumer Blackwell (vLLM #21471)
- [Phase 01-readme]: README uses paper-abstract structure to communicate research-stage project with hedging language
- [Phase 01-readme]: Three Mermaid diagrams chosen for architecture section (renders natively on GitHub)
- [Phase 01-readme]: Tensor parallelism explicitly excluded in Hardware section with PCIe bottleneck rationale
</additional_context>

<output>
Write to: .planning/phases/02-implementation-plan/02-RESEARCH.md
</output>

1→# Welcome to ElixirTrials  Template
     2→
     3→This is a mono-repo template combining Python backend services, LangGraph AI agents, and a React/Vite HITL frontend.
     4→
     5→## 🚀 Quick Start
     6→
     7→### Prerequisites
     8→- **Python 3.12+**
     9→- **Node.js 20+**
    10→- **uv** (Python package manager)
    11→- **Docker**
    12→
    13→### Installation
    14→Run the following commands to get started:
    15→
    16→```bash
    17→# Sync dependencies
    18→uv sync
    19→
    20→# Create a new service (interactive)
    21→make create-service
    22→```
    23→
    24→## 🎯 Project Goals
    25→- **Robust Template**: A production-ready foundation for AI applications.
    26→- **Unified Tooling**: Standardized patterns for backend, frontend, and AI agents.
    27→- **Rapid Development**: Pre-configured CI/CD, linting, and testing.
    28→
    29→## 🏗️ Architecture
    30→
    31→- **API Service**: FastAPI orchestrator handling requests and persistence.
    32→- **Agent Services**: Independent LangGraph agents for specific workflows.
    33→- **Inference**: Shared library for model loading and LLM interactions.
    34→- **Data Pipeline**: ETL processes for data ingestion.
    35→- **Evaluation**: Offline metrics and benchmarks.
    36→- **HITL UI**: React/Vite frontend for human review and approval.
    37→
    38→## 📚 Documentation Index
    39→
    40→- **[Components](components-overview.md)**: Detailed docs for all services and libraries.
    41→- **[Diagrams](diagrams/hitl-flow.md)**: Visual flows of the system.
    42→

# Phase 2: Implementation Plan - Context

**Gathered:** 2026-03-02
**Status:** Ready for planning

<domain>
## Phase Boundary

Write a detailed, phased implementation plan that transforms the Rune architecture specification into actionable phases with pass/fail criteria, risk gates, and a concrete build order grounded in hardware reality. Output is documentation (Markdown files served via MkDocs), not code. The plan covers the full journey from hardware validation through hypernetwork training.

</domain>

<decisions>
## Implementation Decisions

### Plan granularity
- 5-6 coarse implementation phases (not 8-10 fine-grained ones)
- Each phase gets detailed treatment: goal paragraph, rationale for ordering, deliverables, dependencies, risk callouts, and success criteria (~30-40 lines per phase)
- No time estimates — ordering and dependencies only. Timelines are speculative at research stage.
- Single kill-switch gate at Phase 1 (hypothesis validation). Later phases have success criteria but are not hard stops.

### Document structure
- Hybrid tone: engineering spec for phase structure (deliverables, criteria, dependencies) with paper-adjacent framing for context and rationale sections
- Main document + appendices approach: paper-adjacent main plan document plus separate engineering spec appendices (risk matrix, build order details)
- Use MkDocs (already in the repo) to serve all documentation
- Remove existing template/placeholder docs from MkDocs — leave a clean build with only real Rune documentation
- Executive summary first: 1-page overview (goal, phase count, key risks, build order), then detailed phase-by-phase breakdown
- Visual aids: Mermaid dependency graph showing phase ordering, plus tables for build order and risk matrix

### Success criteria style
- Mix of metrics and checklists: specific numbers where measurable (e.g., 5% Pass@1), binary checklists where not (e.g., "adapter registry stores and retrieves")
- Kill-switch gate: primary metric is Pass@1 improvement on HumanEval, with secondary diagnostic signals (adapter convergence, training loss) tracked via MLflow
- Use MLflow for experiment tracking throughout
- Include experiment sketches per phase: dataset, baseline, eval method, expected range — enough to start, not full protocols
- Failed criteria = stop and reassess. No predefined fallback strategies. Document what was learned, revisit approach.

### Claude's Discretion
- Exact phase boundaries and what goes in each phase (within the 5-6 phase constraint)
- Mermaid diagram layout and detail level
- How to structure the MkDocs nav for the new documentation
- Balance of detail between main document and appendices
- Specific experiment sketch parameters (dataset sizes, baseline choices)

</decisions>

<specifics>
## Specific Ideas

- MkDocs is already configured in the repo — use it natively, don't add a separate docs framework
- Template/placeholder docs from the monorepo scaffold should be removed so the docs site only contains real Rune documentation
- MLflow for experiment tracking is a firm choice — reference it in the kill-switch phase and any phase with measurable criteria
- The README (Phase 1 output) already establishes the paper-adjacent voice and Mermaid diagram conventions — the implementation plan should feel like a natural continuation

</specifics>

<deferred>
## Deferred Ideas

None — discussion stayed within phase scope

</deferred>

</phase_context>

<additional_context>
**Phase description:** ### Phase 2: Implementation Plan
**Goal**: A detailed, phased implementation plan document exists that transforms the Rune architecture specification into actionable phases with pass/fail criteria, risk gates, and a concrete build order grounded in hardware reality
**Depends on**: Phase 1
**Requirements**: PLAN-01, PLAN-02, PLAN-03, PLAN-04, PLAN-05, PLAN-06
**Success Criteria** (what must be TRUE):
  1. Phase 0 of the implementation plan defines hardware and environment validation with specific pass/fail criteria (both GPUs recognized, PyTorch nightly cu128 forward+backward pass without segfault, vLLM serving Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via pipeline parallelism without TP+LoRA corruption)
  2. Phase 1 of the implementation plan is structured as a kill-switch gate: it defines a measurable success threshold (at minimum, 5% Pass@1 improvement on held-out HumanEval tasks) and states explicitly that no infrastructure is built if this gate fails
  3. Every subsequent phase in the plan states its deliverables, which previous phase it depends on, and the success criteria that mark it complete
  4. The plan explicitly addresses the three primary research risks: adapter composition interference (heterogeneous subspaces), hypernetwork mode collapse (mean-adapter collapse), and catastrophic forgetting (non-immutable adapter writes) — each with a mitigation strategy
  5. The plan reflects the hardware reality: dual RTX 4090 with CXL, pipeline parallelism (PP=2) not tensor parallelism, QLoRA as required for 24GB per-GPU constraint, with QLoRA introduced only after Phase 1 bfloat16 baseline passes
  6. The plan includes the recommended build order: adapter-registry → lora-server → model-training extensions → api-service extensions → rune-agent → evolution-svc → training-svc → hypernetwork

**Phase requirement IDs (MUST address):** PLAN-01, PLAN-02, PLAN-03, PLAN-04, PLAN-05, PLAN-06

**Requirements:**
- PLAN-01: Phase 0 defines hardware/environment validation with specific pass/fail criteria
- PLAN-02: Phase 1 defines core hypothesis validation as kill-switch gate (Doc-to-LoRA on coding tasks)
- PLAN-03: Each subsequent phase has clear deliverables, dependencies, and success criteria
- PLAN-04: Plan addresses key research risks: adapter composition interference, hypernetwork mode collapse, catastrophic forgetting
- PLAN-05: Plan reflects hardware reality: dual RTX 4090 + CXL, pipeline parallelism (not tensor), QLoRA required
- PLAN-06: Plan includes suggested build order from architecture research (adapter-registry → lora-server → api-extensions → rune-agent → evolution → training)

**Prior decisions:**
- [Roadmap]: Documentation-first milestone — 3 phases (README, Implementation Plan, Architecture Docs)
- [Roadmap]: Phase 2 kill-switch gate: 5% Pass@1 improvement on HumanEval required before infrastructure build
- [Roadmap]: Pipeline parallelism (PP=2, TP=1) mandated — TP+LoRA confirmed bug on consumer Blackwell (vLLM #21471)
- [Phase 01-readme]: README uses paper-abstract structure to communicate research-stage project with hedging language
- [Phase 01-readme]: Three Mermaid diagrams chosen for architecture section (renders natively on GitHub)
- [Phase 01-readme]: Tensor parallelism explicitly excluded in Hardware section with PCIe bottleneck rationale
</additional_context>

<output>
Write to: .planning/phases/02-implementation-plan/02-RESEARCH.md
</output>

content.content:
1→# Components Overview
     2→
     3→This page provides an overview of all microservices and shared libraries in this monorepo.
     4→
     5→## Available Components
     6→
     7→| Component | Description | Documentation |
     8→| :--- | :--- | :--- |
     9→| **agent-a-service** | This component implements a specific AI agent workflow for interacting with guests. It uses LangGraph to define the flow and state. | [API Reference](agent-a-service/api/index.md) |
    10→| **agent-b-service** | This component implements a guardrailing workflow to validate outputs and ensure safety. | [API Reference](agent-b-service/api/index.md) |
    11→| **api-service** | The API Service is the central orchestrator for the application. It provides HTTP endpoints for the frontend, manages database persistence, and triggers background agent tasks. | [API Reference](api-service/api/index.md) |
    12→| **data-pipeline** | This component handles data ingestion (ETL), normalization, and preparation for the API or training. | [API Reference](data-pipeline/api/index.md) |
    13→| **evaluation** | This component runs offline evaluation benchmarks against your agents. | [API Reference](evaluation/api/index.md) |
    14→| **events-py** | Shared event envelope shapes and helpers used by Python services that publish or consume events. | [API Reference](events-py/api/index.md) |
    15→| **events-ts** | Shared event shapes and helpers used by TypeScript services that publish or consume events. | [API Reference](events-ts/api/index.md) |
    16→| **hitl-ui** | React/Vite application for Human-in-the-Loop workflows. This is where users approve, edit, or reject AI-generated content. | [API Reference](hitl-ui/api/index.md) |
    17→| **inference** | This component acts as the "Standard Library" for AI in this repository. It centralizes model loading, prompt rendering, and agent construction to ensure consistency across all services. | [API Reference](inference/api/index.md) |
    18→| **model-training** | This component handles fine-tuning (LoRA), distillation, or training of custom models. | [API Reference](model-training/api/index.md) |
    19→| **shared** | This component holds code that is strictly **common** to multiple components. This usually includes: | [API Reference](shared/api/index.md) |
    20→| **shared-ts** | This library provides common types (e.g. `Result`) and small utilities used across TypeScript services and apps. | [API Reference](shared-ts/api/index.md) |

- Hybrid tone: engineering spec for phase structure (deliverables, criteria, dependencies) with paper-adjacent framing for context and rationale sections
- Main document + appendices approach: paper-adjacent main plan document plus separate engineering spec appendices (risk matrix, build order details)
- Use MkDocs (already in the repo) to serve all documentation
- Remove existing template/placeholder docs from MkDocs — leave a clean build with only real Rune documentation
- Executive summary first: 1-page overview (goal, phase count, key risks, build order), then detailed phase-by-phase breakdown
- Visual aids: Mermaid dependency graph showing phase ordering, plus tables for build order and risk matrix

### Success criteria style
- Mix of metrics and checklists: specific numbers where measurable (e.g., 5% Pass@1), binary checklists where not (e.g., "adapter registry stores and retrieves")
- Kill-switch gate: primary metric is Pass@1 improvement on HumanEval, with secondary diagnostic signals (adapter convergence, training loss) tracked via MLflow
- Use MLflow for experiment tracking throughout
- Include experiment sketches per phase: dataset, baseline, eval method, expected range — enough to start, not full protocols
- Failed criteria = stop and reassess. No predefined fallback strategies. Document what was learned, revisit approach.

### Claude's Discretion
- Exact phase boundaries and what goes in each phase (within the 5-6 phase constraint)
- Mermaid diagram layout and detail level
- How to structure the MkDocs nav for the new documentation
- Balance of detail between main document and appendices
- Specific experiment sketch parameters (dataset sizes, baseline choices)

</decisions>

<specifics>
## Specific Ideas

- MkDocs is already configured in the repo — use it natively, don't add a separate docs framework
- Template/placeholder docs from the monorepo scaffold should be removed so the docs site only contains real Rune documentation
- MLflow for experiment tracking is a firm choice — reference it in the kill-switch phase and any phase with measurable criteria
- The README (Phase 1 output) already establishes the paper-adjacent voice and Mermaid diagram conventions — the implementation plan should feel like a natural continuation

</specifics>

<deferred>
## Deferred Ideas

None — discussion stayed within phase scope

</deferred>

</phase_context>

# Phase 2: Implementation Plan - Research

**Researched:** 2026-03-02
**Domain:** Technical writing — phased implementation plan documentation for a research-stage ML system
**Confidence:** HIGH — output is a documentation artifact; all technical content grounded in verified existing research files

---

<user_constraints>
## User Constraints (from CONTEXT.md)

### Locked Decisions

**Plan granularity:**
- 5-6 coarse implementation phases (not 8-10 fine-grained ones)
- Each phase gets detailed treatment: goal paragraph, rationale for ordering, deliverables, dependencies, risk callouts, and success criteria (~30-40 lines per phase)
- No time estimates — ordering and dependencies only. Timelines are speculative at research stage.
- Single kill-switch gate at Phase 1 (hypothesis validation). Later phases have success criteria but are not hard stops.

**Document structure:**
- Hybrid tone: engineering spec for phase structure (deliverables, criteria, dependencies) with paper-adjacent framing for context and rationale sections
- Main document + appendices approach: paper-adjacent main plan document plus separate engineering spec appendices (risk matrix, build order details)
- Use MkDocs (already in the repo) to serve all documentation
- Remove existing template/placeholder docs from MkDocs — leave a clean build with only real Rune documentation
- Executive summary first: 1-page overview (goal, phase count, key risks, build order), then detailed phase-by-phase breakdown
- Visual aids: Mermaid dependency graph showing phase ordering, plus tables for build order and risk matrix

**Success criteria style:**
- Mix of metrics and checklists: specific numbers where measurable (e.g., 5% Pass@1), binary checklists where not (e.g., "adapter registry stores and retrieves")
- Kill-switch gate: primary metric is Pass@1 improvement on HumanEval, with secondary diagnostic signals (adapter convergence, training loss) tracked via MLflow
- Use MLflow for experiment tracking throughout
- Include experiment sketches per phase: dataset, baseline, eval method, expected range — enough to start, not full protocols
- Failed criteria = stop and reassess. No predefined fallback strategies. Document what was learned, revisit approach.

### Claude's Discretion

- Exact phase boundaries and what goes in each phase (within the 5-6 phase constraint)
- Mermaid diagram layout and detail level
- How to structure the MkDocs nav for the new documentation
- Balance of detail between main document and appendices
- Specific experiment sketch parameters (dataset sizes, baseline choices)

### Deferred Ideas (OUT OF SCOPE)

None — discussion stayed within phase scope
</user_constraints>

---

<phase_requirements>
## Phase Requirements

| ID | Description | Research Support |
|----|-------------|-----------------|
| PLAN-01 | Phase 0 defines hardware/environment validation with specific pass/fail criteria | Hardware pass/fail criteria enumerated from STACK.md and PITFALLS.md; exact validation steps identified |
| PLAN-02 | Phase 1 defines core hypothesis validation as kill-switch gate (Doc-to-LoRA on coding tasks) | Kill-switch framing, 5% Pass@1 threshold, and MLflow tracking all verified from PITFALLS.md and ROADMAP.md |
| PLAN-03 | Each subsequent phase has clear deliverables, dependencies, and success criteria | Full 5-phase structure proposed; each phase has deliverable lists, dependency arrows, and mixed metric/checklist criteria |
| PLAN-04 | Plan addresses key research risks: adapter composition interference, hypernetwork mode collapse, catastrophic forgetting | All three risks with mitigations sourced from PITFALLS.md; risk matrix structure designed for appendix |
| PLAN-05 | Plan reflects hardware reality: dual RTX 4090 + CXL, pipeline parallelism (not tensor), QLoRA required | Hardware constraints from STACK.md and ARCHITECTURE.md; PP=2 mandate and QLoRA deferral confirmed |
| PLAN-06 | Plan includes suggested build order from architecture research | Full 8-step build order from ARCHITECTURE.md; mapped to phase structure |
</phase_requirements>

---

## Summary

This phase is a writing task, not an engineering task. The output is a set of Markdown documentation files served via MkDocs — an implementation plan document (the main artifact) plus at least one appendix. All technical content already exists in the planning research files. This phase distills that content into a structured plan that a developer can follow.

The primary challenge is structural: the plan must be simultaneously readable as an engineering document (deliverables, criteria, dependencies) and credible as a research-stage plan (honest about hypothesis status, not overclaiming). The kill-switch gate at Phase 1 is the critical framing choice — it determines how the rest of the plan is organized. Phases after the gate should feel contingent on the gate passing, not presumed.

The secondary challenge is MkDocs configuration. The existing mkdocs.yml references template services (agent-a-service, agent-b-service, data-pipeline, evaluation, events-py, events-ts, hitl-ui, inference, model-training, shared, shared-ts) that are placeholder content unrelated to Rune. These must be removed from the nav, and the new implementation plan documents must be added. The index.md and other template docs also need to be replaced or removed.

**Primary recommendation:** Write the plan as a five-phase structure (Phase 0 through Phase 4), with the implementation plan document as the main nav entry and the risk matrix and build order as appendices. Clean the MkDocs nav of all template/ElixirTrials content before building.

---

## Standard Stack

### Core

This phase produces documentation. No new library dependencies are introduced. The only tooling consideration is MkDocs, which is already installed and configured.

| Tool | Version | Purpose | Why Standard |
|------|---------|---------|--------------|
| MkDocs | Already installed (mkdocs.yml present) | Static docs site generator | Already in repo; user decision is to use it natively |
| Material theme or default MkDocs theme | Current (mkdocs theme: mkdocs) | Documentation presentation | Current config uses `name: mkdocs` (default theme) |
| Mermaid (CDN) | 11.x (already loaded via CDN in mkdocs.yml) | Dependency graph diagram | Already configured in extra_javascript with mermaid.min.js |
| pymdownx.superfences | Already installed | Mermaid fenced block rendering | Already in mkdocs.yml markdown_extensions |
| mkdocs-monorepo-plugin | Already installed | Sub-nav via `!include` directives | Already in plugins section |

**MkDocs Mermaid rendering note:** The current mkdocs.yml uses `pymdownx.superfences` with a custom fence for Mermaid that renders as `<div class="mermaid">`. The mermaid-init.js script initializes it with `startOnLoad: true`. This is the correct pattern for MkDocs + Mermaid — no changes needed to the existing rendering setup.

**MkDocs fenced block syntax for Mermaid (verified from existing config):**
```yaml
pymdownx.superfences:
  custom_fences:
    - name: mermaid
      class: mermaid
      format: !!python/name:pymdownx.superfences.fence_div_format
```
This means Mermaid diagrams in MkDocs use the same ` ```mermaid ` syntax as GitHub. Diagrams authored for this plan will render both in MkDocs and on GitHub without modification.

### Supporting Libraries

No additional libraries are needed. This phase is entirely documentation.

---

## Architecture Patterns

### Recommended Document Structure

The plan output is two files served via MkDocs:

```
docs/
├── implementation-plan.md       # Main plan document (the primary artifact)
└── appendices/
    ├── risk-matrix.md           # Engineering spec appendix: risk table
    └── build-order.md           # Engineering spec appendix: detailed build order
```

MkDocs nav entry (to replace template nav):
```yaml
nav:
- Home: index.md
- Implementation Plan: implementation-plan.md
- Appendices:
  - Risk Matrix: appendices/risk-matrix.md
  - Build Order: appendices/build-order.md
```

**What to remove from the existing nav:**
- All `!include ./services/*/mkdocs.yml` entries (template services that don't exist for Rune)
- Diagrams section (template diagrams, not Rune-relevant)
- Onboarding: onboarding.md (template content)
- Components: components-overview.md (template content)
- Testing Guide: testing-guide.md (template content — can be kept if useful, but is template content)

**What to update:**
- `docs/index.md` — replace ElixirTrials welcome content with a Rune landing page that introduces the documentation site and links to the implementation plan
- `mkdocs.yml` — update site_name, site_description, site_author, repo_name, repo_url to Rune values

### Pattern 1: Executive Summary → Detailed Breakdown Structure

**What:** Main plan document opens with a compact executive summary (the "1-page overview" user decision), then presents detailed phase-by-phase sections.

**Structure:**
```
implementation-plan.md
├── ## Executive Summary
│   ├── Goal (1-2 sentences)
│   ├── Phase overview table (Phase, Goal, Kill-switch status)
│   ├── Key risks (3 bullets)
│   ├── Build order (Mermaid dependency diagram)
│   └── One-paragraph framing note
│
├── ## Phase 0: Hardware and Environment Validation
│   ├── Goal paragraph
│   ├── Rationale for ordering
│   ├── Deliverables (checklist)
│   ├── Dependencies (none — first phase)
│   ├── Risk callouts
│   ├── Success criteria (pass/fail checklist)
│   └── Experiment sketch (if applicable)
│
├── ## Phase 1: Core Hypothesis Validation (Kill-Switch Gate)
│   ├── Goal paragraph
│   ├── Rationale for ordering (why before infrastructure)
│   ├── Deliverables (checklist)
│   ├── Dependencies (Phase 0 must pass)
│   ├── Risk callouts (the primary research risk)
│   ├── Kill-switch: success threshold (5% Pass@1 on HumanEval)
│   ├── Kill-switch: what failure means (stop and reassess — no predefined fallback)
│   ├── Secondary diagnostic signals (adapter convergence, training loss)
│   └── Experiment sketch (dataset, baseline, eval method, expected range)
│
├── ## Phase 2: Adapter Library and Serving Infrastructure
├── ## Phase 3: Recursive Agent Loop and Sandbox Integration
├── ## Phase 4: Evolution Operator and Adapter Lifecycle
│   (phases 2-4 follow same structure without kill-switch framing)
│
└── ## References
    (link to papers that back claims in the plan)
```

### Pattern 2: Phase Section Template (~30-40 lines each)

Each phase section follows this consistent template:

```markdown
## Phase N: [Name]

**Depends on:** Phase N-1 passes success criteria

[2-3 sentence goal paragraph: what this phase achieves and why it matters for the overall system]

### Why This Phase Comes Here

[1-2 paragraphs explaining the ordering rationale: what capability this unlocks, what risk it mitigates, why earlier phases must complete first]

### Deliverables

- [ ] [Concrete artifact 1]
- [ ] [Concrete artifact 2]
- [ ] [Concrete artifact 3]
...

### Risk Callouts

> **[Risk Name]:** [1-2 sentence description of the risk and its mitigation in this phase]

### Success Criteria

| Criterion | Type | Target |
|-----------|------|--------|
| [measurable thing] | Metric | [specific number] |
| [binary thing] | Checklist | Passes / Fails |

### Experiment Sketch

**Dataset:** [what data, approximate size]
**Baseline:** [what you compare against]
**Evaluation method:** [how you measure]
**Expected range:** [what success looks like, what failure looks like]
```

### Pattern 3: Kill-Switch Gate Framing

Phase 1 requires distinct framing from all other phases. The kill-switch must be:
1. Stated explicitly — "No infrastructure is built if this gate fails"
2. Measurable — primary metric (5% Pass@1 on HumanEval) plus secondary diagnostics
3. Honest about failure — "If the gate fails, stop and document what was learned. The plan does not prescribe a fallback; the failure itself is informative and narrows the design space."

The README already established this framing in the "Current Status" section: "the project is structured so that early phases validate the hypothesis before infrastructure is built." The plan makes this operational.

**MLflow integration for the kill-switch phase:**
- All experiment runs tracked in MLflow with: run_id, phase, adapter_id, pass@1, training_loss, adapter_cosine_diversity
- The kill-switch decision is documented as an MLflow note on the run: "Gate PASSED" or "Gate FAILED — reassessing"
- MLflow artifact storage for the adapter files used in the hypothesis test

### Pattern 4: Mermaid Dependency Graph (Executive Summary)

The executive summary should include a Mermaid flowchart showing phase dependencies. Recommended structure:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Phase 0\nHardware Validation] --> P1
    P1{Phase 1\nHypothesis Gate} -->|passes| P2
    P1 -->|fails| Stop([Stop and Reassess])
    P2[Phase 2\nAdapter Library\n+ Serving] --> P3
    P3[Phase 3\nRecursive Agent\nLoop] --> P4
    P4[Phase 4\nEvolution Operator\n+ Hypernetwork]
```

Key constraints for this diagram:
- The diamond `{...}` node for Phase 1 visually signals the kill-switch gate
- The `Stop` outcome must be present — not hiding the failure path
- Use `flowchart TD` (top-down) for phase dependency flow; phases go vertically
- Keep under 10 nodes including terminal nodes

### Anti-Patterns to Avoid

- **Timelines:** Do not include time estimates. They are speculative at the research stage (locked decision).
- **Fallback strategies:** Do not prescribe what to do if Phase 1 fails. "Stop and reassess" is the correct answer. Inventing a fallback strategy gives false confidence.
- **Overlapping phases:** Do not split infrastructure builds across adjacent phases in a way that creates ambiguous dependencies. Each phase should deliver something independently testable.
- **Hypothesis in Phase 2+:** Do not repeat the core hypothesis validation logic in later phases. The kill-switch is in Phase 1. Later phases assume it passed.
- **Template content remnants:** All ElixirTrials template content in MkDocs (index.md, onboarding.md, components-overview.md, diagrams, testing-guide.md) should be replaced or removed. Do not leave placeholder pages in the nav.
- **Serving template mkdocs.yml files via !include:** The existing nav includes `!include` entries for services that do not exist in Rune (agent-a-service, data-pipeline, etc.). Removing these is required — the build will fail or warn on missing includes.

---

## Don't Hand-Roll

| Problem | Don't Build | Use Instead | Why |
|---------|-------------|-------------|-----|
| Mermaid diagrams | SVG, PNG, ASCII art | Mermaid fenced blocks | MkDocs already renders them; same syntax as GitHub README |
| Risk matrix | Prose risk descriptions scattered through phases | Risk matrix table in appendix | User decision: appendix approach; tables are scannable |
| Build order detail | Repeating build steps in every phase | Separate appendix: build-order.md | Phases reference the appendix; keeps phase sections at ~30-40 lines |
| Experiment protocols | Full experimental methodology | Experiment sketches (~5-7 lines per phase) | Locked decision: "enough to start, not full protocols" |
| Fallback strategies | Pre-planned pivots if Phase 1 fails | Nothing — "stop and reassess" | Locked decision: no predefined fallbacks |

**Key insight:** The plan is a documentation artifact, not a specification. It should be prescriptive about what to build and what order, but not so detailed that it crowds out the engineering judgment needed during implementation. Each phase's experiment sketch should enable a competent engineer to start the work, not replace the work.

---

## Common Pitfalls

### Pitfall 1: MkDocs Build Failure from Missing !include Targets

**What goes wrong:** Running `mkdocs build` or `mkdocs serve` fails with errors like `FileNotFoundError: services/agent-a-service/mkdocs.yml does not exist`.

**Why it happens:** The existing nav in mkdocs.yml uses `!include` directives pointing to per-service mkdocs.yml files. The monorepo-plugin resolves these at build time. If the nav is not updated to remove the !include entries, the build fails.

**How to avoid:** Before adding new nav entries, remove all `!include` entries for services that no longer have documentation (agent-a-service, agent-b-service, data-pipeline, evaluation, events-py, events-ts, hitl-ui, inference, model-training, shared, shared-ts). Verify `mkdocs build` succeeds without errors after the nav update.

**Warning signs:** `mkdocs serve` exits with FileNotFoundError or KeyError during plugin resolution.

### Pitfall 2: MkDocs Strict Mode Validation Errors

**What goes wrong:** MkDocs warns about `omitted_files`, `absolute_links`, `unrecognized_links`, or `anchors` — these are set to `warn` in the existing mkdocs.yml (validation section). If the docs reference pages that do not exist yet (e.g., `docs/appendices/risk-matrix.md` linked before the file is created), warnings appear.

**Why it happens:** The existing mkdocs.yml has `validation: omitted_files: warn` and `validation: absolute_links: warn`. These are warnings, not errors, but they should be addressed.

**How to avoid:** Create all documentation files before updating the nav. Do not add nav entries for files that are not yet written. Verify with `mkdocs build` after each file creation.

**Warning signs:** MkDocs outputs `WARNING - Doc file ... not found` or `WARNING - Unrecognized link` during build.

### Pitfall 3: Phase Sections Growing Past ~40 Lines

**What goes wrong:** The phase-by-phase detail sections become 80-120 lines each, making the main plan document too long to scan.

**Why it happens:** Each risk callout wants more explanation, each deliverable wants sub-bullets, experiment sketches expand into full protocols.

**How to avoid:** Apply the ~30-40 line budget per phase strictly. Overflow goes into appendices (risk matrix) or into separate architecture docs (Phase 3 which documents component details). The experiment sketch is the most common overflow point — keep it to 5-7 lines.

**Warning signs:** Phase 1 section exceeds 50 lines; the experiment sketch becomes a full test plan.

### Pitfall 4: Implied Timelines

**What goes wrong:** Language like "this phase takes approximately 2-3 weeks" or "after completing Phase 0, Phase 1 can begin immediately" implies timeline estimates.

**Why it happens:** Technical writers instinctively add time guidance. The user explicitly locked this out.

**How to avoid:** Use dependency language, not timeline language. "Phase 1 begins when Phase 0 success criteria are met" not "Phase 1 begins 1-2 weeks after Phase 0." Search the document for "week", "month", "day", "hours", "takes approximately" before finalizing.

**Warning signs:** Any temporal quantity in the text.

### Pitfall 5: Phase 0 Conflating Hardware Validation with Environment Setup

**What goes wrong:** Phase 0 grows to include "install PyTorch" and "create virtual environment" — becoming a setup guide rather than a hardware validation gate.

**Why it happens:** Hardware validation naturally leads to environment setup, and the writer includes both.

**How to avoid:** Phase 0 is a validation gate, not an installation guide. The success criteria should be binary tests that either pass or fail: "both GPUs recognized by CUDA" (pass/fail), "PyTorch nightly cu128 forward+backward pass without segfault" (pass/fail), "vLLM serves Qwen2.5-Coder-7B via PP=2 without TP+LoRA corruption" (pass/fail). The installation steps that achieve these are pre-conditions or references to setup docs, not the deliverables.

**Warning signs:** Phase 0 includes `uv pip install` commands as deliverables.

### Pitfall 6: Mermaid Rendering Differences Between MkDocs and GitHub

**What goes wrong:** The dependency graph renders correctly in MkDocs but not on GitHub (or vice versa).

**Why it happens:** MkDocs uses `pymdownx.superfences` to wrap the mermaid block in `<div class="mermaid">` and initializes it via JavaScript. GitHub renders `mermaid` fenced blocks natively using its own renderer. Both support the same basic flowchart syntax, but some edge cases differ (e.g., `classDef` styling is partially supported on GitHub, not at all in basic Mermaid.js rendering).

**How to avoid:** Use only basic flowchart syntax: node shapes (`[]`, `{}`, `([])`, `[()]`), edge types (`-->`, `-->|label|`), and direction (`flowchart TD`). Do not use `classDef`, `style`, `linkStyle`, or `subgraph`. Test both by viewing the file in GitHub preview and running `mkdocs serve`. The diagrams in the existing README (authored during Phase 1) followed these constraints and render correctly.

**Warning signs:** Using any syntax beyond basic nodes and edges.

---

## Code Examples

This phase produces no executable code. All outputs are Markdown files.

### MkDocs Nav Configuration Pattern

The updated `mkdocs.yml` nav should follow this structure (replacing the existing template nav):

```yaml
nav:
- Home: index.md
- Implementation Plan: implementation-plan.md
- Appendices:
  - Risk Matrix: appendices/risk-matrix.md
  - Build Order: appendices/build-order.md
```

Note: The `mkdocstrings` plugin is currently configured with `paths: [services/*/src, libs/*/src]`. This is safe to keep (it does not fail if no docstrings exist), but the nav must not reference non-existent service mkdocs.yml files.

### MkDocs Site Metadata Update

The existing mkdocs.yml has ElixirTrials template metadata. Update these fields:

```yaml
site_name: Rune Documentation
site_description: Implementation plan and architecture documentation for Rune
site_author: [project author]
site_url: [if publishing; omit or leave as-is for local development]
repo_name: [github-username]/rune
repo_url: https://github.com/[github-username]/rune
```

### Risk Matrix Table Pattern (Appendix)

The risk matrix appendix should use a consistent table format:

```markdown
| Risk | Phase | Severity | Mitigation | Warning Signs |
|------|-------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Adapter composition interference | Phase 2 | Medium | Default to single-adapter retrieval; treat multi-adapter composition as optional experiment | Performance with 2 adapters worse than 1 |
| Hypernetwork mode collapse | Phase 1 | High | Diversity regularization in training loss; monitor adapter cosine diversity metric | Generated adapters cluster tightly in PCA |
| Catastrophic forgetting | Phase 2 | Medium | Immutable adapter storage; write-once semantics enforced at registry API | Performance on session-1 tasks degrades after session-50 adapters added |
```

### Build Order Table Pattern (Appendix)

```markdown
| Step | Component | Depends On | What It Unblocks |
|------|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | libs/adapter-registry | Nothing | All components that store or retrieve adapters |
| 2 | services/lora-server | adapter-registry | All agent work; hypothesis testing |
| 3 | libs/model-training (extend) | lora-server | training-svc |
| 4 | services/api-service (extend) | adapter-registry | hitl-ui integration |
| 5 | services/rune-agent | lora-server, api-service, sandbox | Core recursive loop |
| 6 | services/evolution-svc | adapter-registry, lora-server | Adapter lifecycle management |
| 7 | services/training-svc | adapter-registry, model-training | LoRA fine-tuning jobs |
| 8 | Hypernetwork | training-svc, adapter corpus | Doc-to-LoRA inference-time synthesis |
```

---

## Recommended Phase Structure

This is the critical content decision (within Claude's discretion): how to structure 5-6 phases. The following five-phase structure is recommended, grounded in the architecture research and locked decisions.

### Phase 0: Hardware and Environment Validation

**Goal:** Confirm the hardware and software environment is ready for GPU-dependent work. This is a precondition gate, not a research gate.

**Key deliverables:**
- Both RTX 4090 GPUs recognized by CUDA (both in `nvidia-smi` output)
- PyTorch nightly cu128 forward+backward pass completes without segfault on both GPUs
- vLLM serves Qwen2.5-Coder-7B with `--pipeline-parallel-size 2 --tensor-parallel-size 1` without crash
- vLLM serves a known-good adapter and produces correct output (regression test)
- PEFT + bitsandbytes QLoRA fine-tune runs 1 training step without error

**Hardware constraints to document explicitly:**
- `--pipeline-parallel-size 2, --tensor-parallel-size 1` — required; TP+LoRA is a confirmed vLLM bug (#21471)
- `VLLM_FLASH_ATTN_VERSION=2` — Flash Attention 3 does not support Blackwell consumer GPUs (RTX 4090)
- PyTorch must be nightly cu128 — stable PyTorch 2.7/2.8 lacks cu128 wheels

**Success criteria style:** Binary pass/fail checklist. No metrics.

### Phase 1: Core Hypothesis Validation (Kill-Switch Gate)

**Goal:** Determine empirically whether the Doc-to-LoRA approach can encode coding trajectories into useful LoRA adapters. No infrastructure is built until this passes.

**Key deliverables:**
- Minimal Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork trained on 50-100 coding trajectory pairs
- Adapter quality evaluation on held-out HumanEval tasks (not full HumanEval — a held-out subset)
- MLflow run documenting: Pass@1 vs baseline, training loss curve, adapter cosine diversity, ||ΔW|| norms
- Written assessment: what passed, what failed, what was learned

**Hardware note:** Run the baseline in bfloat16 (not QLoRA) to isolate hypothesis from quantization artifacts. QLoRA is introduced in Phase 2 after the baseline passes.

**Experiment sketch:**
- Dataset: 50-100 real coding trajectory pairs from HumanEval or SWE-bench-lite; each trajectory = (task description, attempt sequence, final passing code)
- Baseline: Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct with no adapter (raw model Pass@1)
- Eval method: Pass@1 on a 20-30 task held-out subset of HumanEval; run 5 samples per task
- Expected range: 5-15% improvement over baseline signals a real effect; < 5% improvement = gate fails

**Kill-switch:** If Pass@1 improvement < 5% on held-out tasks, the project stops and reassesses. No predefined fallback. The failure is documented as a research finding.

**Secondary diagnostic signals** (tracked via MLflow, not gating criteria):
- Adapter cosine diversity > 0.1 across training batch (detecting mode collapse)
- Training loss converges and does not plateau in first 5% of budget
- ||ΔW|| is meaningfully nonzero (adapter is not being ignored)

### Phase 2: Adapter Library and Serving Infrastructure

**Goal:** Build the component foundation that all subsequent phases depend on: the adapter registry, the vLLM server with dynamic LoRA loading, and the API service extensions.

**Key deliverables:**
- `libs/adapter-registry`: SQLModel schema, write-once enforcement, path resolution
- `services/lora-server`: vLLM Dockerfile, startup script (PP=2, TP=1, enable-lora), basic health check
- `services/api-service` extensions: `/adapters` and `/sessions` routes; SQLModel tables
- QLoRA integration: bfloat16 baseline → NF4 QLoRA transition with quality comparison logged to MLflow

**Primary risk callout:** Adapter composition interference. Default must be single-adapter-per-request at inference time. Multi-adapter composition is an optional future experiment, not the default.

**Primary risk callout:** Catastrophic forgetting. The adapter registry must enforce write-once semantics at the storage API level. No code path may overwrite an existing adapter.

**Success criteria:** Mix of checklists (adapter registry stores/retrieves correctly; vLLM server starts and serves with dynamic LoRA loading; no TP=2 flag anywhere in config) and metrics (QLoRA quality vs bfloat16 baseline < 10% degradation on held-out HumanEval tasks; logged in MLflow).

### Phase 3: Recursive Agent Loop and Sandbox Integration

**Goal:** Build the core agent loop (`services/rune-agent`) and the sandboxed code execution environment. This phase produces the first end-to-end path from task to adapter.

**Key deliverables:**
- `services/rune-agent`: LangGraph StateGraph with `generate → execute → reflect → save` cycle
- Sandbox integration: Docker-based code execution with network isolation, memory/CPU limits
- Adapter selection: query adapter-registry at session start, load most-relevant adapter into lora-server
- End-to-end test: task → agent loop → successful code → trajectory captured → passed to distillation
- `libs/model-training` extensions: PEFT utilities, trajectory-to-adapter fine-tuning script

**Success criteria:** Checklists. Binary pass/fail for each integration point. No metrics in this phase — the agent loop producing valid adapters is the success.

### Phase 4: Evolution Operator and Hypernetwork

**Goal:** Build the adapter lifecycle management system (`services/evolution-svc`) and the Doc-to-LoRA hypernetwork inference path (`services/training-svc` hypernetwork job). This is the final phase and depends on having a corpus of adapters produced by Phase 3.

**Key deliverables:**
- `services/evolution-svc`: fitness evaluation, tournament selection, pruning, adapter promotion
- `services/training-svc`: hypernetwork training job; hypernetwork inference (single forward pass → adapter)
- Adapter hierarchy: project-root, domain, task-specific levels populated by evolution
- MLflow tracking: adapter fitness scores, evolution events, hypernetwork reconstruction loss

**Primary risk callout:** Hypernetwork mode collapse. Diversity regularization must be in the training loss from the start. Monitor adapter cosine diversity metric — if it falls below threshold, training stops.

**Primary risk callout:** Minimum corpus size for hypernetwork training. The hypernetwork requires a diverse corpus of pre-trained adapters. If Phase 3 has not produced enough adapters (< 50-100 diverse pairs), delay hypernetwork training and continue accumulating adapters.

**Success criteria:** Mix of metrics (hypernetwork reconstruction loss on held-out adapters; adapter cosine diversity > threshold) and checklists (evolution operator promotes, prunes, and archives adapters without corrupting the registry; hypernetwork produces adapters that load and run without error in vLLM).

---

## State of the Art

| Old Approach | Current Approach | When Changed | Impact for This Plan |
|--------------|------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| All phases presented as equally certain | Phase 1 as explicit kill-switch gate | Research project management best practice | Plan explicitly stops at Phase 1 if hypothesis fails |
| Time-boxed phases | Dependency-ordered phases, no time estimates | User decision for this project | Phases are contingent, not scheduled |
| Infrastructure first, hypothesis second | Hypothesis first, infrastructure contingent | Learned from failed ML projects (RAND 2024 research) | Adapter registry and agent loop are only built if Phase 1 passes |
| Tensor parallelism for multi-GPU | Pipeline parallelism (PP=2, TP=1) | vLLM issue #21471, confirmed RTX consumer Blackwell limitation | Plan mandates PP=2 in all phases that reference multi-GPU serving |
| bfloat16 throughout | bfloat16 for baseline, QLoRA introduced after hypothesis validates | QLoRA paper recommendation; isolate variables | Phase 1 runs in bfloat16; Phase 2 introduces QLoRA with explicit comparison |

---

## Open Questions

1. **MkDocs theme: keep default or upgrade to Material?**
   - What we know: Current config uses `theme: name: mkdocs` (default MkDocs theme)
   - What's unclear: Whether to upgrade to Material for MkDocs (richer features, admonitions, better nav)
   - Recommendation: Keep the default theme. The user decision is to use MkDocs natively without adding a new framework. Material is a separate install and a separate decision. Document the current default theme as-is.

2. **Exact HumanEval subset size for Phase 1 kill-switch evaluation**
   - What we know: HumanEval has 164 tasks; 5% Pass@1 improvement is the threshold; the plan says "held-out subset"
   - What's unclear: How many tasks constitute a statistically meaningful held-out subset for the kill-switch decision
   - Recommendation: Use 20-30 tasks as the held-out subset. At 5 samples per task this is 100-150 evaluations — sufficient for a directional result without being a full benchmark run. Specify this in the experiment sketch for Phase 1 (Claude's discretion area).

3. **MkDocs docs_dir — keep `docs/` or create `docs/rune/`?**
   - What we know: Current config: `docs_dir: docs`; files go in `docs/`
   - What's unclear: Whether to create a subdirectory to separate Rune docs from template remnants
   - Recommendation: Use the existing `docs/` directory. Create `docs/appendices/` as a new subdirectory for the appendix files. Replace template files (index.md) in-place. Keep the directory structure flat.

4. **How to handle existing docs files (onboarding.md, testing-guide.md, components-overview.md)**
   - What we know: These are template content, not Rune content. User decision: remove template placeholder docs.
   - What's unclear: Whether to delete the files or just remove them from the nav
   - Recommendation: Remove from the nav only — do not delete the files. MkDocs does not complain about files in docs_dir that are not in the nav (only warns via `omitted_files: warn`). The nav controls what is published; the files can remain as orphans. This is safer than deletion.

5. **site_url in mkdocs.yml**
   - What we know: Current value is `https://elixirtrials-.github.io/template-repo/docs/` — clearly template placeholder
   - What's unclear: Whether GitHub Pages will be used; what the correct URL should be
   - Recommendation: Update site_url to the actual GitHub repository URL, or remove the field if not publishing to GitHub Pages. This does not affect local `mkdocs serve`. Flag as a placeholder-to-update for the planner.

---

## Sources

### Primary (HIGH confidence)

- `.planning/research/ARCHITECTURE.md` — Component build order, data flows, anti-patterns, phase dependencies
- `.planning/research/PITFALLS.md` — Three primary research risks with mitigations; Phase 1 kill-switch criteria; diagnostic signals for mode collapse
- `.planning/research/STACK.md` — Hardware constraints (RTX 4090, PP=2 mandate, QLoRA requirements), version pinning requirements
- `.planning/ROADMAP.md` — Phase 2 success criteria (PLAN-01 through PLAN-06); kill-switch threshold (5% Pass@1); build order
- `.planning/REQUIREMENTS.md` — Requirement IDs and acceptance criteria
- `mkdocs.yml` — Current MkDocs configuration; existing nav structure; plugins; markdown extensions
- `README.md` — Established paper-adjacent voice and Mermaid conventions this plan must continue
- `.planning/phases/01-readme/01-RESEARCH.md` — Mermaid syntax constraints; MkDocs rendering patterns; template content inventory

### Secondary (MEDIUM confidence)

- `.planning/research/FEATURES.md` — Background on Rune's differentiators; informs rationale sections
- MkDocs official docs (general knowledge) — Nav configuration, docs_dir, validation settings

### Tertiary (LOW confidence)

None required — all content for this phase is grounded in existing verified planning artifacts.

---

## Metadata

**Confidence breakdown:**
- Document structure (phases, nav, MkDocs): HIGH — all content grounded in existing, verified planning artifacts; MkDocs config directly inspected
- Phase content (deliverables, success criteria): HIGH — directly sourced from ROADMAP.md success criteria and PITFALLS.md/ARCHITECTURE.md research
- Experiment sketch parameters (dataset sizes, held-out task count): MEDIUM — reasonable estimates; within Claude's discretion area; not empirically sourced
- MkDocs rendering behavior: HIGH — current config directly inspected; mermaid-init.js and pymdownx.superfences both confirmed working

**Research date:** 2026-03-02
**Valid until:** 2026-06-01 (stable; MkDocs configuration is stable; phase structure is determined by locked decisions)

**Key insight for planner:** This phase is a documentation writing task with two distinct concerns: (1) writing the implementation plan content, and (2) cleaning up the MkDocs configuration. These are best handled in sequence — clean the nav first to avoid broken builds, then write the plan documents. The five-phase structure recommended here (Phase 0 through Phase 4) satisfies all PLAN-01 through PLAN-06 requirements. The planner should produce one main task (write implementation-plan.md), two appendix tasks (risk-matrix.md, build-order.md), and one MkDocs cleanup task (update mkdocs.yml and docs/index.md). A fifth task for MkDocs build validation is advisable.

# Phase 2: Implementation Plan - Context

**Gathered:** 2026-03-02
**Status:** Ready for planning

<domain>
## Phase Boundary

Write a detailed, phased implementation plan that transforms the Rune architecture specification into actionable phases with pass/fail criteria, risk gates, and a concrete build order grounded in hardware reality. Output is documentation (Markdown files served via MkDocs), not code. The plan covers the full journey from hardware validation through hypernetwork training.

</domain>

<decisions>
## Implementation Decisions

### Plan granularity
- 5-6 coarse implementation phases (not 8-10 fine-grained ones)
- Each phase gets detailed treatment: goal paragraph, rationale for ordering, deliverables, dependencies, risk callouts, and success criteria (~30-40 lines per phase)
- No time estimates — ordering and dependencies only. Timelines are speculative at research stage.
- Single kill-switch gate at Phase 1 (hypothesis validation). Later phases have success criteria but are not hard stops.

### Document structure
- Hybrid tone: engineering spec for phase structure (deliverables, criteria, dependencies) with paper-adjacent framing for context and rationale sections
- Main document + appendices approach: paper-adjacent main plan document plus separate engineering spec appendices (risk matrix, build order details)
- Use MkDocs (already in the repo) to serve all documentation
- Remove existing template/placeholder docs from MkDocs — leave a clean build with only real Rune documentation
- Executive summary first: 1-page overview (goal, phase count, key risks, build order), then detailed phase-by-phase breakdown
- Visual aids: Mermaid dependency graph showing phase ordering, plus tables for build order and risk matrix

### Success criteria style
- Mix of metrics and checklists: specific numbers where measurable (e.g., 5% Pass@1), binary checklists where not (e.g., "adapter registry stores and retrieves")
- Kill-switch gate: primary metric is Pass@1 improvement on HumanEval, with secondary diagnostic signals (adapter convergence, training loss) tracked via MLflow
- Use MLflow for experiment tracking throughout
- Include experiment sketches per phase: dataset, baseline, eval method, expected range — enough to start, not full protocols
- Failed criteria = stop and reassess. No predefined fallback strategies. Document what was learned, revisit approach.

### Claude's Discretion
- Exact phase boundaries and what goes in each phase (within the 5-6 phase constraint)
- Mermaid diagram layout and detail level
- How to structure the MkDocs nav for the new documentation
- Balance of detail between main document and appendices
- Specific experiment sketch parameters (dataset sizes, baseline choices)

</decisions>

<specifics>
## Specific Ideas

- MkDocs is already configured in the repo — use it natively, don't add a separate docs framework
- Template/placeholder docs from the monorepo scaffold should be removed so the docs site only contains real Rune documentation
- MLflow for experiment tracking is a firm choice — reference it in the kill-switch phase and any phase with measurable criteria
- The README (Phase 1 output) already establishes the paper-adjacent voice and Mermaid diagram conventions — the implementation plan should feel like a natural continuation

</specifics>

<deferred>
## Deferred Ideas

None — discussion stayed within phase scope

</deferred>

</phase_context>

**Phase description:** ### Phase 2: Implementation Plan
**Goal**: A detailed, phased implementation plan document exists that transforms the Rune architecture specification into actionable phases with pass/fail criteria, risk gates, and a concrete build order grounded in hardware reality

  2. Phase 1 of the implementation plan is structured as a kill-switch gate: it defines a measurable success threshold (at minimum, 5% Pass@1 improvement on held-out HumanEval tasks) and states explicitly that no infrastructure is built if this gate fails

  4. The plan explicitly addresses the three primary research risks: adapter composition interference (heterogeneous subspaces), hypernetwork mode collapse (mean-adapter collapse), and catastrophic forgetting (non-immutable adapter writes) — each with a mitigation strategy

- [Phase 01-readme]: Tensor parallelism explicitly excluded in Hardware section with PCIe bottleneck rationale

The research is complete. Here is the structured result:

---

## RESEARCH COMPLETE

**Phase:** 02 - implementation-plan
**Confidence:** HIGH

### Key Findings

- This phase is a pure documentation writing task. All technical content is already verified and grounded in existing planning research files. No new technical research was required.
- The five-phase structure (Phase 0 through Phase 4) satisfies all six PLAN requirements. Phase 0 = hardware validation gate; Phase 1 = kill-switch hypothesis gate; Phases 2-4 = infrastructure and capability builds.

The plan must use RTX 4090 throughout. The vLLM TP+LoRA bug (#21471) still applies regardless of GPU architecture.
